text: As in conventional open surgery we start with the division of the inferior pulmonary ligament.
We continue to divide the ligament until the inferior pulmonary vein has been visualized.
Usually at this point a station 9 lymph node serves as the landmark to identify the inferior pulmonary vein.

The suction which is being held by the operator's left hand plays an important role.
It is usually used to lift up the lung and give adequate traction so that the inferior pulmonary ligament can be divided easily by using energized dissection.
Now we can see that the inferior pulmonary vein has been completely dissected.
We now move on to the anterior part of the fissure.
There seems to be adhesions between the upper and the lower lobe which are being divided.
The fissure here appears to be partially fused.
And we can also see clearly an interlobar node sitting in a crucial juncture.
This node seems to be sitting right above the interlobar vessels, mainly the interlobar PA.
Unless we are able to clear this node of the interlobar PA and identify its branches, we will not be able to proceed in a timely fashion.
These densely adherent nodes that you can be seen here on your screen are very common in our part of the world.
And anybody wishing to practice minimally invasive thoracic surgery is going to be troubled by it.
As explained earlier, this node is at a very crucial juncture.
In order to get an R0 resection, we need to dissect this node off completely.
If we are unable to do it, and if the node is positive for metastasis, to achieve an R0 resection,
you might actually be needing a left pneumonectomy.
During such instances, it is our usual practice to send such nodes for frozen section analysis.
After meticulously dissecting in the surrounding areas, we finally seem to have found a plane to dissect the node of the surrounding vessels and expose the arteries underneath.
Now we go on and divide the posterior part of the
pleura surrounding the hilum.
Division of this pleura serves two purposes.
One, it creates enough space to pass a stapler to divide the fissure as well as the vessels.
And second, it facilitates nodal dissection.
As you can see here, the posterior hilar nodes, which are part of station 10, are being cleared.
Before we proceed further, I would like to briefly review the anatomy.
As you can see on your screen here, there are two branches from the interlobar artery going into the upper lobe.
These are most probably the A4 and the A5.
They are the two lingular branches arising separately from the interlobar artery.
Then we move on to the anterior part of the fissure and divide the parenchyma with a stapler.
Now in a similar fashion, we move on to the posterior part of the fissure.
Before dividing the posterior part of the fissure, we make sure that the artery to segment 6 and the posterior recurrent artery are well away.
Now as you can see here, before we pass a stapler, we need to create enough space between the vessels and the posterior part of the fissure, which is being done using a dissector.
Once we create enough space, then a stapler is used to divide the lung parenchyma.
Then we move on to the vascular dissection.
First, we dissect branches of the interlobar artery.
The first branch that we need to delineate and preserve is the lingular branch of the interlobar artery.
As shown earlier, there are two lingular branches separately arising from the interlobar artery.
Once both the lingular arteries are dissected completely free, we go on and divide the bacillar artery.
But here we notice that there is something peculiar about the anatomy.
The lingular artery seems to have taken off at a lower point than usually and there is very little space between the bacillar artery and the takeoff of the lingular artery.
So we have used a thin curved tip stapler and angled the stapler in such a way as to preserve both the lingular arteries.
Our next step would be to identify the apicobasal artery that is the artery to segment 6.
Here we have to also note that there are multiple posterior branches supplying to the upper lobe which need to be delineated and for that this interlobar node needs to be completely removed.
Here we continue to dissect this node of the interlobar artery so that we can expose the entire length of the interlobar artery and identify all the branches to the lower lobe and divide them safely.
As is well described, there are usually two branches to the lower lobe, the apicobasal artery and the basilar trunk.
As it is well known, the left upper lobe is known to have a variant anatomy and multiple small branches directly arising from the interlobar artery.

So before we perform a left lower lobectomy, it is important to keep in mind that we need to extensively dissect the entire length of the interlobar artery and identify all the branches applying to the upper lobe so that none of them get compromised.
Before we go on and divide any further arteries, we would like to briefly review the anatomy.
As you can see here, the entire length of the interlobar has been dissected.
You can see the apicobasal, the branches to the upper lobe and the two lingular arteries.
Once we review the anatomy and we know that we are safe, we go on and divide the apicobasal artery to the lower lobe.
You can see here a curved tip stapler is being passed across and the apical basal artery is being divided.
Then we proceed to go on and divide the inferior pulmonary vein.
Now we are demonstrating the subcarinal nodal dissection.
The subcarinal area is particularly challenging in the left side because it lies at a depth and it lies posterior to the cardia.
Subcarinal dissection usually proceeds parallel to the left main bronchus.
The surgeon goes on dissecting the nodes until he reaches the carina.
Usually in this area, there is a consistent branch of the bronchial artery which can cause troublesome bleeding.
If possible, it is better to identify this early and divide it.
As the final step of the procedure, we go on and divide the lower lobar bronchus.
Before we divide, it is our usual practice to inflate the lung and confirm that we are dividing the right bronchus and then we extract the specimen using a plastic bag.
An intercostal nerve block is given as part of multimodal analgesia.
